[[File:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tōtomi Province|ja|遠江国}} {{ill|Sano District|ja|佐野郡}} {{ill|Tomibe Village|ja|富部村}}’s Lending Bowl Legend as depicted in a color painting from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} (from the edition of “Wangan Ike” by {{ill|Fujichōkō|ja|藤長庚}} in the {{ill|Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja|遠江古蹟圖會}}, 1803; held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}})]] '''Lending Bowl Legend''' (わんかしでんせつ) is a collective term for a type of folk tale and traditional narrative – one of the narrative types of folklore – whose central theme is a story in which meal trays ({{ill|Meal Tray|ja|膳}}) or bowls ({{ill|Bowl|ja|椀}}) are borrowed from mounds, abysses, large rocks, or caves in the shady parts of mountains.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, p.230</ref>
== Examples of the Lending Bowl Legend == {{Quote|In a place called Kanaya, there was a large rock, and when one shouted beside that rock, “Lend me meal trays and bowls for as many people as I need,” the next morning the desired number of trays and bowls were prepared. One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}} {{ill|Hattō District|ja|八頭郡}}’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}
== Overview == In Japan prior to the early modern period, families did not possess more tableware than was necessary for the household; consequently, during events that gathered large numbers of people, such as weddings, it was not uncommon for meal trays and bowls to be borrowed from elsewhere.
The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽}} to {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the {{ill|Chūbu region|ja|中部地方}} and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子}}, {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童}}, {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍}}, {{ill|Goddess|ja|神 (神道)|lt=Goddess|lt_ja=女神}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ({{ill|Abyss|ja|淵}}), waterfalls ({{ill|Waterfall|ja|滝}}), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ({{ill|Hidden Village|ja|隠れ里}}). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚}} and the {{ill|Water Deity|ja|水神}}. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}, {{ill|Inba District|ja|印旛郡}}, {{ill|Sakae Town|ja|栄町}} (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群}} in {{ill|Shisui Town|ja|酒々井町}}, have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the {{ill|Mochishi|ja|木地師}}, leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of {{ill|Silent Trade|ja|沈黙交易|lt=Silent Trade|lt_ja=無言貿易}} with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist {{ill|Ryūzō Torii|ja|鳥居龍蔵}} pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a {{ill|Deity|ja|神}}, it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, {{ill|Shinichirō Kurimoto|ja|栗本慎一郎}} argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year {{ill|Masao Oka|ja|岡正雄}} argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In {{ill|France|ja|フランス}}, there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a {{ill|Pot|ja|鍋}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *{{Cite book|和書|last=神野|first=清|chapter=印波国造の奥津城|title=龍女建立-龍角寺古墳群と龍角寺-|publisher={{ill|Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|ja|千葉県立房総のむら}}|year=2009|date=2009|ncid=BB00830428|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群}}
{{Japan-culture-stub}} {{citation needed|わんかしてんせつ}}

== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In {{ill|France|ja|フランス}}, there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a {{ill|Pot|ja|鍋}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *{{Cite book|和書|last=神野|first=清|chapter=印波国造の奥津城|title=龍女建立-龍角寺古墳群と龍角寺-|publisher={{ill|Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|ja|千葉県立房総のむら}}|year=2009|date=2009|ncid=BB00830428|ref=harv}}== See Also == * {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群}}

{{!-- Interlanguage and Category Links --}} [[ja:%E6%A4%80%E8%B2%B8%E4%BC%9D%E8%AA%AC]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] <!--Lending Bowl Legend--> [[Category:Lending Bowl Legend]] [[Category:Lending Bowl Legend]] [[Category:Lending Bowl Legend]] [[en:Lending Bowl Legend]] [[en:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] <!--Lending Bowl Legend--> [[Category:11]][[en:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:ending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]]
[[File:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tōtomi Province|ja|遠江国}} {{ill|Sano District|ja|佐野郡}} {{ill|Tomibe Village|ja|富部村}}’s Lending Bowl Legend as depicted in a color painting from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} (from the edition of “Wangan Ike” by {{ill|Fujichōkō|ja|藤長庚}} in the {{ill|Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja|遠江古蹟圖會}}, 1803; held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}})]] '''Lending Bowl Legend''' (わんかしでんせつ) is a collective term for a type of folk tale and traditional narrative – one of the narrative types of folklore – whose central theme is a story in which meal trays ({{ill|Meal Tray|ja|膳}}) or bowls ({{ill|Bowl|ja|椀}}) are borrowed from mounds, abysses, large rocks, or caves in the shady parts of mountains.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, p.230</ref>
== Examples of the Lending Bowl Legend == {{Quote|In a place called Kanaya, there was a large rock, and when one shouted beside that rock, “Lend me meal trays and bowls for as many people as I need,” the next morning the desired number of trays and bowls were prepared. One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}} {{ill|Hattō District|ja|八頭郡}}’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}
== Overview == In Japan prior to the early modern period, families did not possess more tableware than was necessary for the household; consequently, during events that gathered large numbers of people, such as weddings, it was not uncommon for meal trays and bowls to be borrowed from elsewhere.
The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽}} to {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the {{ill|Chūbu region|ja|中部地方}} and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子}}, {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童}}, {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍}}, {{ill|Goddess|ja|神 (神道)|lt=Goddess|lt_ja=女神}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ({{ill|Abyss|ja|淵}}), waterfalls ({{ill|Waterfall|ja|滝}}), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ({{ill|Hidden Village|ja|隠れ里}}). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚}} and the {{ill|Water Deity|ja|水神}}. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}, {{ill|Inba District|ja|印旛郡}}, {{ill|Sakae Town|ja|栄町}} (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群}} in {{ill|Shisui Town|ja|酒々井町}}, have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the {{ill|Mochishi|ja|木地師}}, leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of {{ill|Silent Trade|ja|沈黙交易|lt=Silent Trade|lt_ja=無言貿易}} with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist {{ill|Ryūzō Torii|ja|鳥居龍蔵}} pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a {{ill|Deity|ja|神}}, it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, {{ill|Shinichirō Kurimoto|ja|栗本慎一郎}} argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year {{ill|Masao Oka|ja|岡正雄}} argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In {{ill|France|ja|フランス}}, there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a {{ill|Pot|ja|鍋}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *{{Cite book|和書|last=神野|first=清|chapter=印波国造の奥津城|title=龍女建立-龍角寺古墳群と龍角寺-|publisher={{ill|Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|ja|千葉県立房総のむら}}|year=2009|date=2009|ncid=BB00830428|ref=harv}}== See Also == * {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群}}
{{Japan-culture-stub}} {{citation needed|わんかしてんせつ}}

== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of {{ill|Silent Trade|ja|沈黙交易|lt=Silent Trade|lt_ja=無言貿易}} with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist {{ill|Ryūzō Torii|ja|鳥居龍蔵}} pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a {{ill|Deity|ja|神}}, it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, {{ill|Shinichirō Kurimoto|ja|栗本慎一郎}} argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year {{ill|Masao Oka|ja|岡正雄}} argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In {{ill|France|ja|フランス}}, there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a {{ill|Pot|ja|鍋}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *{{Cite book|和書|last=神野|first=清|chapter=印波国造の奥津城|title=龍女建立-龍角寺古墳群と龍角寺-|publisher={{ill|Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|ja|千葉県立房総のむら}}|year=2009|date=2009|ncid=BB00830428|ref=harv}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群}}
{{Japan-culture-stub}} {{citation needed|わんかしてんせつ}}

{{!-- Interlanguage and Category Links --}} [[ja:%E6%A4%80%E8%B2%B8%E4%BC%9D%E8%AA%AC]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] <!--Lending Bowl Legend--> [[Category:Lending Bowl Legend]] [[Category:Lending Bowl Legend]] [[Category:Lending Bowl Legend]] [[en:Lending Bowl Legend]] [[en:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] <!--Lending Bowl Legend--> [[Category:11]][[en:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:ending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Lending Bowl Legend]][[Category:11]]
[[File:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tōtomi Province|ja|遠江国}} {{ill|Sano District|ja|佐野郡}} {{ill|Tomibe Village|ja|富部村}}’s Lending Bowl Legend as depicted in a color painting from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} (from the edition of “Wangan Ike” by {{ill|Fujichōkō|ja|藤長庚}} in the {{ill|Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja|遠江古蹟圖會}}, 1803; held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}})]] '''Lending Bowl Legend''' (わんかしでんせつ) is a collective term for a type of folk tale and traditional narrative – one of the narrative types of folklore – whose central theme is a story in which meal trays ({{ill|Meal Tray|ja|膳}}) or bowls ({{ill|Bowl|ja|椀}}) are borrowed from mounds, abysses, large rocks, or caves in the shady parts of mountains.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, p.230</ref>
== Examples of the Lending Bowl Legend == {{Quote|In a place called Kanaya, there was a large rock, and when one shouted beside that rock, “Lend me meal trays and bowls for as many people as I need,” the next morning the desired number of trays and bowls were prepared. One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}} {{ill|Hattō District|ja|八頭郡}}’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}
== Overview == In Japan prior to the early modern period, families did not possess more tableware than was necessary for the household; consequently, during events that gathered large numbers of people, such as weddings, it was not uncommon for meal trays and bowls to be borrowed from elsewhere.
The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽}} to {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the {{ill|Chūbu region|ja|中部地方}} and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子}}, {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童}}, {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍}}, {{ill|Goddess|ja|神 (神道)|lt=Goddess|lt_ja=女神}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ({{ill|Abyss|ja|淵}}), waterfalls ({{ill|Waterfall|ja|滝}}), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ({{ill|Hidden Village|ja|隠れ里}}). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚}} and the {{ill|Water Deity|ja|水神}}. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}, {{ill|Inba District|ja|印旛郡}}, {{ill|Sakae Town|ja|栄町}} (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群}} in {{ill|Shisui Town|ja|酒々井町}}, have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the {{ill|Mochishi|ja|木地師}}, leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of {{ill|Silent Trade|ja|沈黙交易|lt=Silent Trade|lt_ja=無言貿易}} with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist {{ill|Ryūzō Torii|ja|鳥居龍蔵}} pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a {{ill|Deity|ja|神}}, it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, {{ill|Shinichirō Kurimoto|ja|栗本慎一郎}} argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year {{ill|Masao Oka|ja|岡正雄}} argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In {{ill|France|ja|フランス}}, there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a {{ill|Pot|ja|鍋}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *{{Cite book|和書|last=神野|first=清|chapter=印波国造の奥津城|title=龍女建立-龍角寺古墳群と龍角寺-|publisher={{ill|Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|ja|千葉県立房総のむら}}|year=2009|date=2009|ncid=BB00830428|ref=harv}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群}}
{{Japan-culture-stub}} {{citation needed|わんかしてんせつ}}

Among these items, some hint at a connection with the {{ill|Mochishi|ja|木地師|12=draft}}, leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=draft}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>

In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist {{ill|Ryūzō Torii|ja|鳥居龍蔵|12=draft}} pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=draft}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a {{ill|Deity|ja|神|12=draft}}, it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=draft}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, {{ill|Shinichirō Kurimoto|ja|栗本慎一郎|12=draft}} argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year {{ill|Masao Oka|ja|岡正雄|12=draft}} argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In {{ill|France|ja|フランス|12=draft}}, there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a {{ill|Pot|ja|鍋|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *{{Cite book|和書|last=神野|first=清|chapter=印波国造の奥津城|title=龍女建立-龍角寺古墳群と龍角寺-|publisher={{ill|Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|ja|千葉県立房総のむら|12=draft}}|year=2009|date=2009|ncid=BB00830428|ref=harv}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=draft}}

revision.text:
{{!-- Interlanguage and Category Links --}} <!--Lending Bowl Legend-->
<!--Lending Bowl Legend-->
[[File:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tōtomi Province|ja|遠江国|12=draft}} {{ill|Sano District|ja|佐野郡|12=draft}} {{ill|Tomibe Village|ja|富部村|12=draft}}’s Lending Bowl Legend as depicted in a color painting from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}} (from the edition of “Wangan Ike” by {{ill|Fujichōkō|ja|藤長庚|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja|遠江古蹟圖會|12=draft}}, 1803; held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館|12=draft}})]] '''Lending Bowl Legend''' (わんかしでんせつ) is a collective term for a type of folk tale and traditional narrative – one of the narrative types of folklore – whose central theme is a story in which meal trays ({{ill|Meal Tray|ja|膳|12=draft}}) or bowls ({{ill|Bowl|ja|椀|12=draft}}) are borrowed from mounds, abysses, large rocks, or caves in the shady parts of mountains.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, p.230</ref>
== Examples of the Lending Bowl Legend == {{Quote|In a place called Kanaya, there was a large rock, and when one shouted beside that rock, “Lend me meal trays and bowls for as many people as I need,” the next morning the desired number of trays and bowls were prepared. One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|12=draft}} {{ill|Hattō District|ja|八頭郡|12=draft}}’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}
== Overview == In Japan prior to the early modern period, families did not possess more tableware than was necessary for the household; consequently, during events that gathered large numbers of people, such as weddings, it was not uncommon for meal trays and bowls to be borrowed from elsewhere.
The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽|12=draft}} to {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|12=draft}}—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the {{ill|Chūbu region|ja|中部地方|12=draft}} and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東|12=draft}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kappa|ja|河童|12=draft}}, {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|12=draft}}, {{ill|Goddess|ja|神 (神道)|lt=Goddess|lt_ja=女神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|12=draft}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ({{ill|Abyss|ja|淵|12=draft}}), waterfalls ({{ill|Waterfall|ja|滝|12=draft}}), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|12=draft}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ({{ill|Hidden Village|ja|隠れ里|12=draft}}). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Water Deity|ja|水神|12=draft}}. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=draft}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Inba District|ja|印旛郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sakae Town|ja|栄町|12=draft}} (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=draft}} in {{ill|Shisui Town|ja|酒々井町|12=draft}}, have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|12=draft}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the {{ill|Mochishi|ja|木地師|12=draft}}, leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=draft}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of {{ill|Silent Trade|ja|沈黙交易|lt=Silent Trade|lt_ja=無言貿易|12=draft}} with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist {{ill|Ryūzō Torii|ja|鳥居龍蔵|12=draft}} pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=draft}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a {{ill|Deity|ja|神|12=draft}}, it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=draft}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, {{ill|Shinichirō Kurimoto|ja|栗本慎一郎|12=draft}} argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year {{ill|Masao Oka|ja|岡正雄|12=draft}} argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In {{ill|France|ja|フランス|12=draft}}, there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a {{ill|Pot|ja|鍋|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *{{Cite book|和書|last=神野|first=清|chapter=印波国造の奥津城|title=龍女建立-龍角寺古墳群と龍角寺-|publisher={{ill|Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|ja|千葉県立房総のむら|12=draft}}|year=2009|date=2009|ncid=BB00830428|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=draft}}
{{Japan-culture-stub}} {{citation needed|わんかしてんせつ}}

Among these items, some hint at a connection with the {{ill|Mochishi|ja|木地師|12=draft}}, leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=draft}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of {{ill|Silent Trade|ja|沈黙交易|lt=Silent Trade|lt_ja=無言貿易|12=draft}} with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist {{ill|Ryūzō Torii|ja|鳥居龍蔵|12=draft}} pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=draft}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a {{ill|Deity|ja|神|12=draft}}, it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=draft}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, {{ill|Shinichirō Kurimoto|ja|栗本慎一郎|12=draft}} argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year {{ill|Masao Oka|ja|岡正雄|12=draft}} argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In {{ill|France|ja|フランス|12=draft}}, there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a {{ill|Pot|ja|鍋|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *{{Cite book|和書|last=神野|first=清|chapter=印波国造の奥津城|title=龍女建立-龍角寺古墳群と龍角寺-|publisher={{ill|Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|ja|千葉県立房総のむら|12=draft}}|year=2009|date=2009|ncid=BB00830428|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=draft}}
{{Japan-culture-stub}} {{citation needed|わんかしてんせつ}}

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|[[遠江国]][[佐野郡]]富部村の椀貸伝説を描いた[[江戸時代]]の彩色画（「湾岸池」[[藤長庚]]編集『[[遠江古蹟圖會]]』1803年。[[国立国会図書館]]蔵）]] '''椀貸伝説'''（わんかしでんせつ）とは、[[民話]]・[[伝承]]の[[物語の類型|類型]]の一つで、塚や淵、大岩、山陰の洞穴などから[[膳]]や[[椀]]を借りる話を主題とした言い伝えの総称である<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房、1977年、第14刷、p.230</ref>。
== 椀貸伝説の例 == {{Quote|金屋という場所に大きな岩があり、その岩の側で「膳椀を何人分貸してくれ」と叫ぶと、翌朝には希望した数の膳と椀が用意されていた。ある時、不心得者が借りた椀の数をごまかして返したところ、2度と貸してもらえなくなった。|[[鳥取県]] [[八頭郡]]の伝承|『昔話伝説小事典』みずうみ書房 1987年、p.273}}
== 概要 == [[近世]]以前の日本では、家に家族に必要な数以上の食器を持たなかったため、[[婚礼]]など人数が集まる催しの際に余所から膳や椀を借りるという状況はしばしば発生した。
椀貸伝説は[[奥羽]]から[[九州]]まで日本の広い範囲に伝搬しているが、特に[[中部地方]]や[[北関東]]の山沿いなどに多く伝わっている。概ね上記の例と同じ筋書きだが小異は多く、貸してくれる相手は[[童子]]や[[河童]]、[[龍]]、[[神 (神道)|女神]]、[[お地蔵様]]や、上記の事例のように正体不明であったり様々である。
貸してくれる場所は[[淵]]や[[滝]]、岩や山陰の[[洞穴]]、[[隠れ里]]に直接取りに行く場合もあり、やはり様々である。比較すると水に因む場所が多く、[[水神少童譚]]や[[水神]]信仰との関連が考えられる伝説も多い。物語には既に膳椀を貸してもらえる関係ができている場合や、椀が川の上流から流れてくるなどして異界や隠れ里を発見する場合もある。[[千葉県]][[印旛郡]][[栄町]]の[[龍角寺岩屋古墳]]や{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}、同県同郡[[酒々井町]]の[[カンカンムロ横穴群]]<ref name=Shisui2>{{Cite web|和書|author=酒々井町風土記|url=https://www.town.shisui.chiba.jp/static/chunk0001/fuudoki/page28/page28.html|title=28.厳島のカンカンムロ|work=酒々井町教育委員会|accessdate=2020-08-22}}</ref>など、[[古墳]]の[[横穴式石室]]や[[横穴墓]]が伝説地となっている事例もある。なお酒々井町（カンカンムロ）の事例では、最初に碗貸しを祈願する相手は[[弁財天]]だが、実際に碗や膳を貸してくる横穴墓の中にいる存在は正体不明である。
この伝説には、不心得者が返さなかったとされる椀や、反故にした証文などが残っている家や地域がある。それらの品々の中には[[木地師]]との関係をうかがわせるものがあり、木地師との交易の際に聞いた[[口上]]が伝説になったという見方がある<ref>『神話伝説辞典』東京堂出版、1968年、第6刷、pp.471-472</ref>。また、膳椀を村の共有財産としている地域では、借りたものを盗むな、壊すなという戒めを含んだ説話であるという見方もある<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房、1977年、第14刷、pp.238-239</ref>。
== 「沈黙交易」説を巡って == 椀貸伝説が異族との[[沈黙交易|無言貿易]]を表したものだという説には民俗学者の中で賛否がある<ref>柳田國男監修『民俗学辞典』東京堂出版、1975年、第48刷、pp.689-690</ref>。
[[1917年]]（[[大正]]6年）に[[人類学者]]の[[鳥居龍蔵]]が『[[人類学雑誌]]』において、椀貸伝説を当事者が接触せず言葉を交わさずに[[交易]]を行う「沈黙交易（''Silent trade''の[[訳語]]」であると指摘した。これに対して[[1918年]]（大正7年）には[[柳田国男]]が『東京日日新聞』に「隠れ里」を発表し、椀貸伝説は貸借関係に過ぎず、さらに相手は[[神]]であることから信仰現象であるとし、竜宮伝説や[[隠れ里]]伝説など「異郷観念」の表現形態であると論じた。
戦後には[[1954年]]（昭和29年）に[[北見俊夫]]が日本全国の椀貸伝説の事例を集成しつつ、これを「沈黙交易」とすることを否定している。一方で、[[1979年]]には[[栗本慎一郎]]が『経済人類学』において椀貸伝説は「沈黙交易」であり、交易の原始的形態であるとしている。
「沈黙交易」を「交易の原初的形態」と見る点に関しては、同年に[[岡正雄]]が『異人その他』において「沈黙交易」は交易の原始的形態ではなく交換の特殊型に過ぎず、客人歓待を前提とした「好意的贈答」の習慣であるとした。
== 世界の椀貸伝説 == [[フランス]]には塚に頼んで[[鍋]]を借りる話がある。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
==参考図書== *{{Cite book|和書|last=神野|first=清|chapter=印波国造の奥津城|title=龍女建立-龍角寺古墳群と龍角寺-|publisher=[[千葉県立房総のむら]]|year=2009|date=2009|ncid=BB00830428|ref=harv}}
==関連項目== *[[カンカンムロ横穴群]]
{{Japan-culture-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:わんかしてんせつ}} [[Category:文化人類学]] [[Category:日本の伝説]] [[Category:神話類型]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24862355]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|[[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtomi Province]] {{ill|Sano District|ja|佐野郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sano District|qq=|1=Saya district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11385111}} {{ill|Tomibe Village|ja|富部村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomibe Village|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s Lending Bowl Legend as depicted in a color painting from the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (from the edition of “Wangan Ike” by {{ill|Fujichōkō|ja|藤長庚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujichōkō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}} in the {{ill|Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja|遠江古蹟圖會|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}}, 1803; held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]])]] '''Lending Bowl Legend''' (椀貸伝説, Wankashidensetsu) is a collective term for a type of folk tale and traditional narrative – one of the narrative types of folklore – whose central theme is a story in which meal trays ({{ill|Zen (container)|ja|膳|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meal Tray|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11612404|lt=Zen}}) or bowls ({{ill|Bowl|ja|椀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bowl|qq=|1=wan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540470}}) are borrowed from mounds, abysses, large rocks, or caves in the shady parts of mountains.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, p.230</ref>
== Examples of the Lending Bowl Legend == {{Quote|In a place called Kanaya, there was a large rock, and when one shouted beside that rock, “Lend me meal trays and bowls for as many people as I need,” the next morning the desired number of trays and bowls were prepared. One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|[[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]] [[:en:Yazu District, Tottori|Hattō District]]’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}
== Overview == In Japan prior to the early modern period, families did not possess more tableware than was necessary for the household; consequently, during events that gathered large numbers of people, such as weddings, it was not uncommon for meal trays and bowls to be borrowed from elsewhere.
The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okuwa|qq=|1=Ōu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446093}} to [[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]]—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Kanto|qq=|1=Kita Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366730}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Child Deity|qq=|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, [[:en:Kappa (folklore)|Kappa]], {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竜|1=dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7559}}, [[:en:Kami|Goddess]], {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ([[:en:Stream pool|Abyss]]), waterfalls ([[:en:Waterfall|Waterfall]]), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洞窟|1=cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q35509}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ([[:en:Hidden village|Hidden Village]]). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Suijin|water god]]. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Inba District|Inba District]], [[:en:Sakae, Chiba|Sakae Town]] (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|qq=|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in [[:en:Shisui|Shisui Town]], have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|qq=|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the [[:en:Kijiya|Mochishi]], leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of [[:en:Silent trade|Silent Trade]] with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist [[:en:Torii Ryūzō|Ryūzō Torii]] pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]], it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|qq=|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, [[:en:Shinichiro Kurimoto|Shinichirō Kurimoto]] argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year [[:en:Masao Oka|Masao Oka]] argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In [[:en:France|France]], there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a [[:en:Stock pot|Pot]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24862355]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|[[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtomi Province]] {{ill|Sano District|ja|佐野郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sano District|qq=|1=Saya district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11385111}} {{ill|Tomibe Village|ja|富部村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomibe Village|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s Lending Bowl Legend as depicted in a color painting from the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (from the edition of “Wangan Ike” by {{ill|Fujichōkō|ja|藤長庚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujichōkō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}} in the {{ill|Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja|遠江古蹟圖會|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}}, 1803; held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]])]] '''Lending Bowl Legend''' (椀貸伝説, Wankashidensetsu) is a collective term for a type of folk tale and traditional narrative – one of the narrative types of folklore – whose central theme is a story in which meal trays ({{ill|Zen (container)|ja|膳|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meal Tray|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11612404|lt=Zen}}) or bowls ({{ill|Bowl|ja|椀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bowl|qq=|1=wan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540470}}) are borrowed from mounds, abysses, large rocks, or caves in the shady parts of mountains.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, p.230</ref>
== Examples of the Lending Bowl Legend == {{Quote|In a place called Kanaya, there was a large rock, and when one shouted beside that rock, “Lend me meal trays and bowls for as many people as I need,” the next morning the desired number of trays and bowls were prepared. One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|[[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]] [[:en:Yazu District, Tottori|Hattō District]]’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}
== Overview == In Japan prior to the early modern period, families did not possess more tableware than was necessary for the household; consequently, during events that gathered large numbers of people, such as weddings, it was not uncommon for meal trays and bowls to be borrowed from elsewhere.
The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okuwa|qq=|1=Ōu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446093}} to [[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]]—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Kanto|qq=|1=Kita Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366730}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Child Deity|qq=|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, [[:en:Kappa (folklore)|Kappa]], {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竜|1=dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7559}}, [[:en:Kami|Goddess]], {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ([[:en:Stream pool|Abyss]]), waterfalls ([[:en:Waterfall|Waterfall]]), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洞窟|1=cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q35509}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ([[:en:Hidden village|Hidden Village]]). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Suijin|water god]]. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Inba District|Inba District]], [[:en:Sakae, Chiba|Sakae Town]] (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|qq=|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in [[:en:Shisui|Shisui Town]], have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|qq=|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the [[:en:Kijiya|Mochishi]], leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>

In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist [[:en:Torii Ryūzō|Ryūzō Torii]] pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]], it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|qq=|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, [[:en:Shinichiro Kurimoto|Shinichirō Kurimoto]] argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year [[:en:Masao Oka|Masao Oka]] argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.

The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okuwa|1=Ōu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446093}} to [[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]]—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Kanto|1=Kita Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366730}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Child Deity|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, [[:en:Kappa (folklore)|Kappa]], {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竜|1=dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7559}}, [[:en:Kami|Goddess]], {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ([[:en:Stream pool|Abyss]]), waterfalls ([[:en:Waterfall|Waterfall]]), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洞窟|1=cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q35509}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ([[:en:Hidden village|Hidden Village]]). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Suijin|water god]]. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Inba District|Inba District]], [[:en:Sakae, Chiba|Sakae Town]] (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in [[:en:Shisui|Shisui Town]], have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the [[:en:Kijiya|Mochishi]], leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of [[:en:Silent trade|Silent Trade]] with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist [[:en:Torii Ryūzō|Ryūzō Torii]] pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]], it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, [[:en:Shinichiro Kurimoto|Shinichirō Kurimoto]] argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year [[:en:Masao Oka|Masao Oka]] argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In [[:en:France|France]], there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a [[:en:Stock pot|Pot]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24862355]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|[[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtomi Province]] {{ill|Sano District|ja|佐野郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sano District|1=Saya district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11385111}} {{ill|Tomibe Village|ja|富部村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomibe Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s Lending Bowl Legend as depicted in a color painting from the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (from the edition of “Wangan Ike” by {{ill|Fujichōkō|ja|藤長庚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujichōkō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}} in the {{ill|Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja|遠江古蹟圖會|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}}, 1803; held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]])]] '''Lending Bowl Legend''' (椀貸伝説, Wankashidensetsu) is a collective term for a type of folk tale and traditional narrative – one of the narrative types of folklore – whose central theme is a story in which meal trays ({{ill|Zen (container)|ja|膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meal Tray|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11612404|lt=Zen}}) or bowls ({{ill|Bowl|ja|椀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bowl|1=wan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540470}}) are borrowed from mounds, abysses, large rocks, or caves in the shady parts of mountains.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, p.230</ref>
== Examples of the Lending Bowl Legend == {{Quote|In a place called Kanaya, there was a large rock, and when one shouted beside that rock, “Lend me meal trays and bowls for as many people as I need,” the next morning the desired number of trays and bowls were prepared. One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|[[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]] [[:en:Yazu District, Tottori|Hattō District]]’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}
== Overview == In Japan prior to the early modern period, families did not possess more tableware than was necessary for the household; consequently, during events that gathered large numbers of people, such as weddings, it was not uncommon for meal trays and bowls to be borrowed from elsewhere.
The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okuwa|1=Ōu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446093}} to [[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]]—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Kanto|1=Kita Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366730}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Child Deity|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, [[:en:Kappa (folklore)|Kappa]], {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竜|1=dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7559}}, [[:en:Kami|Goddess]], {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ([[:en:Stream pool|Abyss]]), waterfalls ([[:en:Waterfall|Waterfall]]), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洞窟|1=cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q35509}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ([[:en:Hidden village|Hidden Village]]). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Suijin|water god]]. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Inba District|Inba District]], [[:en:Sakae, Chiba|Sakae Town]] (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in [[:en:Shisui|Shisui Town]], have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the [[:en:Kijiya|Mochishi]], leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>

In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist [[:en:Torii Ryūzō|Ryūzō Torii]] pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). On the other hand, in 1979, [[:en:Shinichiro Kurimoto|Shinichirō Kurimoto]] argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year [[:en:Masao Oka|Masao Oka]] argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.

Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Child Deity|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, [[:en:Kappa (folklore)|Kappa]], {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竜|1=dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7559}}, [[:en:Kami|Goddess]], {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ([[:en:Stream pool|Abyss]]), waterfalls ([[:en:Waterfall|Waterfall]]), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洞窟|1=cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q35509}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ([[:en:Hidden village|Hidden Village]]). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Suijin|water god]]. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Inba District|Inba District]], [[:en:Sakae, Chiba|Sakae Town]] (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in [[:en:Shisui|Shisui Town]], have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the [[:en:Kijiya|Mochishi]], leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of [[:en:Silent trade|Silent Trade]] with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist [[:en:Torii Ryūzō|Ryūzō Torii]] pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]], it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, [[:en:Shinichiro Kurimoto|Shinichirō Kurimoto]] argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year [[:en:Masao Oka|Masao Oka]] argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In [[:en:France|France]], there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a [[:en:Stock pot|Pot]].
