Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Suijin|water god]]. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Inba District|Inba District]], [[:en:Sakae, Chiba|Sakae Town]] (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in [[:en:Shisui|Shisui Town]], have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the [[:en:Kijiya|Mochishi]], leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of [[:en:Silent trade|Silent Trade]] with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist [[:en:Torii Ryūzō|Ryūzō Torii]] pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]], it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, [[:en:Shinichiro Kurimoto|Shinichirō Kurimoto]] argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year [[:en:Masao Oka|Masao Oka]] argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In [[:en:France|France]], there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a [[:en:Stock pot|Pot]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24862355]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|[[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtomi Province]] {{ill|Sano District|ja|佐野郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sano District|1=Saya district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11385111}} {{ill|Tomibe Village|ja|富部村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomibe Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s Lending Bowl Legend as depicted in a color painting from the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (from the edition of “Wangan Ike” by {{ill|Fujichōkō|ja|藤長庚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujichōkō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}} in the {{ill|Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja|遠江古蹟圖會|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}}, 1803; held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]])]] '''Lending Bowl Legend''' (椀貸伝説, Wankashidensetsu) is a collective term for a type of folk tale and traditional narrative – one of the narrative types of folklore – whose central theme is a story in which meal trays ({{ill|Zen (container)|ja|膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meal Tray|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11612404|lt=Zen}}) or bowls ({{ill|Bowl|ja|椀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bowl|1=wan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540470}}) are borrowed from mounds, abysses, large rocks, or caves in the shady parts of mountains.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, p.230</ref>
== Examples of the Lending Bowl Legend == {{Quote|In a place called Kanaya, there was a large rock, and when one shouted beside that rock, “Lend me meal trays and bowls for as many people as I need,” the next morning the desired number of trays and bowls were prepared. One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|[[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]] [[:en:Yazu District, Tottori|Hattō District]]’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}
== Overview == In Japan prior to the early modern period, families did not possess more tableware than was necessary for the household; consequently, during events that gathered large numbers of people, such as weddings, it was not uncommon for meal trays and bowls to be borrowed from elsewhere.
The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okuwa|1=Ōu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446093}} to [[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]]—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Kanto|1=Kita Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366730}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Child Deity|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, [[:en:Kappa (folklore)|Kappa]], {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竜|1=dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7559}}, [[:en:Kami|Goddess]], {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ([[:en:Stream pool|Abyss]]), waterfalls ([[:en:Waterfall|Waterfall]]), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洞窟|1=cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q35509}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ([[:en:Hidden village|Hidden Village]]). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Suijin|water god]]. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Inba District|Inba District]], [[:en:Sakae, Chiba|Sakae Town]] (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in [[:en:Shisui|Shisui Town]], have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the [[:en:Kijiya|Mochishi]], leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of [[:en:Silent trade|Silent Trade]] with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist [[:en:Torii Ryūzō|Ryūzō Torii]] pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]], it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, [[:en:Shinichiro Kurimoto|Shinichirō Kurimoto]] argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year [[:en:Masao Oka|Masao Oka]] argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In [[:en:France|France]], there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a [[:en:Stock pot|Pot]].

Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Suijin|water god]]. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Inba District|Inba District]], [[:en:Sakae, Chiba|Sakae Town]] (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in [[:en:Shisui|Shisui Town]], have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the [[:en:Kijiya|Mochishi]], leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of [[:en:Silent trade|Silent Trade]] with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist [[:en:Torii Ryūzō|Ryūzō Torii]] pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]], it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, [[:en:Shinichiro Kurimoto|Shinichirō Kurimoto]] argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year [[:en:Masao Oka|Masao Oka]] argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In [[:en:France|France]], there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a [[:en:Stock pot|Pot]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24862355]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|[[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtomi Province]] {{ill|Sano District|ja|佐野郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sano District|1=Saya district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11385111}} {{ill|Tomibe Village|ja|富部村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomibe Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s Lending Bowl Legend as depicted in a color painting from the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (from the edition of “Wangan Ike” by {{ill|Fujichōkō|ja|藤長庚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujichōkō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}} in the {{ill|Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja|遠江古蹟圖會|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}}, 1803; held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]])]] '''Lending Bowl Legend''' (椀貸伝説, Wankashidensetsu) is a collective term for a type of folk tale and traditional narrative – one of the narrative types of folklore – whose central theme is a story in which meal trays ({{ill|Zen (container)|ja|膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meal Tray|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11612404|lt=Zen}}) or bowls ({{ill|Bowl|ja|椀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bowl|1=wan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540470}}) are borrowed from mounds, abysses, large rocks, or caves in the shady parts of mountains.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, p.230</ref>
== Examples of the Lending Bowl Legend == {{Quote|In a place called Kanaya, there was a large rock, and when one shouted beside that rock, “Lend me meal trays and bowls for as many people as I need,” the next morning the desired number of trays and bowls were prepared. One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|[[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]] [[:en:Yazu District, Tottori|Hattō District]]’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}
== Overview == In Japan prior to the early modern period, families did not possess more tableware than was necessary for the household; consequently, during events that gathered large numbers of people, such as weddings, it was not uncommon for meal trays and bowls to be borrowed from elsewhere.
The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okuwa|1=Ōu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446093}} to [[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]]—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Kanto|1=Kita Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366730}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Child Deity|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, [[:en:Kappa (folklore)|Kappa]], {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竜|1=dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7559}}, [[:en:Kami|Goddess]], {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ([[:en:Stream pool|Abyss]]), waterfalls ([[:en:Waterfall|Waterfall]]), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洞窟|1=cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q35509}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ([[:en:Hidden village|Hidden Village]]). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Suijin|water god]]. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Inba District|Inba District]], [[:en:Sakae, Chiba|Sakae Town]] (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in [[:en:Shisui|Shisui Town]], have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the [[:en:Kijiya|Mochishi]], leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>

In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist [[:en:Torii Ryūzō|Ryūzō Torii]] pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Suijin|water god]]. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Inba District|Inba District]], [[:en:Sakae, Chiba|Sakae Town]] (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in [[:en:Shisui|Shisui Town]], have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the [[:en:Kijiya|Mochishi]], leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of [[:en:Silent trade|Silent Trade]] with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist [[:en:Torii Ryūzō|Ryūzō Torii]] pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]], it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, [[:en:Shinichiro Kurimoto|Shinichirō Kurimoto]] argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year [[:en:Masao Oka|Masao Oka]] argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In [[:en:France|France]], there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a [[:en:Stock pot|Pot]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *{{Cite book|和書|last=神野|first=清|chapter=印波国造の奥津城|title=龍女建立-龍角寺古墳群と龍角寺-|publisher={{ill|Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|ja|千葉県立房総のむら|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|1=Chiba Prefectural Open-air Museum Boso-no-Mura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406280}}|year=2009|date=2009|ncid=BB00830428|ref=harv}}

[[File:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtomi Province}} {{ill|Sano District|ja|佐野郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sano District|1=Saya district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11385111}} {{ill|Tomibe Village|ja|富部村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomibe Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s Lending Bowl Legend as depicted in a color painting from the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (from the edition of “Wangan Ike” by {{ill|Fujichōkō|ja|藤長庚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujichōkō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}} in the {{ill|Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja|遠江古蹟圖會|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}}, 1803; held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library}})]] '''Lending Bowl Legend''' (椀貸伝説, Wankashidensetsu) is a collective term for a type of folk tale and traditional narrative – one of the narrative types of folklore – whose central theme is a story in which meal trays ({{ill|Zen (container)|ja|膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meal Tray|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11612404|lt=Zen}}) or bowls ({{ill|Bowl|ja|椀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bowl|1=wan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540470}}) are borrowed from mounds, abysses, large rocks, or caves in the shady parts of mountains.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, p.230</ref>
== Examples of the Lending Bowl Legend == {{Quote|In a place called Kanaya, there was a large rock, and when one shouted beside that rock, “Lend me meal trays and bowls for as many people as I need,” the next morning the desired number of trays and bowls were prepared. One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture}} {{ill|Yazu District, Tottori|en|Yazu District, Tottori|WD=Q1043163|lt=Hattō District}}’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}
== Overview == In Japan prior to the early modern period, families did not possess more tableware than was necessary for the household; consequently, during events that gathered large numbers of people, such as weddings, it was not uncommon for meal trays and bowls to be borrowed from elsewhere.
The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okuwa|1=Ōu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446093}} to {{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyushu}}—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region}} and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Kanto|1=Kita Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366730}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Child Deity|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, {{ill|Kappa (folklore)|en|Kappa (folklore)|WD=Q335140|lt=Kappa}}, {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竜|1=dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7559}}, {{ill|Kami|en|Kami|WD=Q524158|lt=Goddess}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ({{ill|Stream pool|en|Stream pool|WD=Q2385513|lt=Abyss}}), waterfalls ({{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洞窟|1=cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q35509}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ({{ill|Hidden village|en|Hidden village|WD=Q45263|lt=Hidden Village}}). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god}}. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}}, {{ill|Inba District|en|Inba District|WD=Q677245|lt=Inba District}}, {{ill|Sakae, Chiba|en|Sakae, Chiba|WD=Q1202754|lt=Sakae Town}} (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in {{ill|Shisui|en|Shisui|WD=Q782157|lt=Shisui Town}}, have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the {{ill|Kijiya|en|Kijiya|WD=Q11518247|lt=Mochishi}}, leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of {{ill|Silent trade|en|Silent trade|WD=Q2986252|lt=Silent Trade}} with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist {{ill|Torii Ryūzō|en|Torii Ryūzō|WD=Q707532|lt=Ryūzō Torii}} pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}}, it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, {{ill|Shinichiro Kurimoto|en|Shinichiro Kurimoto|WD=Q7497527|lt=Shinichirō Kurimoto}} argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year {{ill|Masao Oka|en|Masao Oka|WD=Q2017050|lt=Masao Oka}} argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In {{ill|France|en|France|WD=Q142|lt=France}}, there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a {{ill|Stock pot|en|Stock pot|WD=Q15711817|lt=Pot}}.

== Further Reading == *{{Cite book|和書|last=神野|first=清|chapter=印波国造の奥津城|title=龍女建立-龍角寺古墳群と龍角寺-|publisher={{ill|Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|ja|千葉県立房総のむら|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|1=Chiba Prefectural Open-air Museum Boso-no-Mura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406280}}|year=2009|date=2009|ncid=BB00830428|ref=harv}}

One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture}} {{ill|Yazu District, Tottori|en|Yazu District, Tottori|WD=Q1043163|lt=Hattō District}}’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}

The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okuwa|1=Ōu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446093}} to {{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyushu}}—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region}} and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Kanto|1=Kita Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366730}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Child Deity|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, {{ill|Kappa (folklore)|en|Kappa (folklore)|WD=Q335140|lt=Kappa}}, {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竜|1=dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7559}}, {{ill|Kami|en|Kami|WD=Q524158|lt=Goddess}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ({{ill|Stream pool|en|Stream pool|WD=Q2385513|lt=Abyss}}), waterfalls ({{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洞窟|1=cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q35509}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ({{ill|Hidden village|en|Hidden village|WD=Q45263|lt=Hidden Village}}). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god}}. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}}, {{ill|Inba District|en|Inba District|WD=Q677245|lt=Inba District}}, {{ill|Sakae, Chiba|en|Sakae, Chiba|WD=Q1202754|lt=Sakae Town}} (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in {{ill|Shisui|en|Shisui|WD=Q782157|lt=Shisui Town}}, have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the {{ill|Kijiya|en|Kijiya|WD=Q11518247|lt=Mochishi}}, leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of {{ill|Silent trade|en|Silent trade|WD=Q2986252|lt=Silent Trade}} with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist {{ill|Torii Ryūzō|en|Torii Ryūzō|WD=Q707532|lt=Ryūzō Torii}} pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}}, it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, {{ill|Shinichiro Kurimoto|en|Shinichiro Kurimoto|WD=Q7497527|lt=Shinichirō Kurimoto}} argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year {{ill|Masao Oka|en|Masao Oka|WD=Q2017050|lt=Masao Oka}} argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In {{ill|France|en|France|WD=Q142|lt=France}}, there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a {{ill|Stock pot|en|Stock pot|WD=Q15711817|lt=Pot}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]]
[[File:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtomi Province}} {{ill|Sano District|ja|佐野郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sano District|1=Saya district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11385111}} {{ill|Tomibe Village|ja|富部村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomibe Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s Lending Bowl Legend as depicted in a color painting from the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (from the edition of “Wangan Ike” by {{ill|Fujichōkō|ja|藤長庚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujichōkō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}} in the {{ill|Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja|遠江古蹟圖會|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}}, 1803; held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library}})]] '''Lending Bowl Legend''' (椀貸伝説, Wankashidensetsu) is a collective term for a type of folk tale and traditional narrative – one of the narrative types of folklore – whose central theme is a story in which meal trays ({{ill|Zen (container)|ja|膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meal Tray|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11612404|lt=Zen}}) or bowls ({{ill|Bowl|ja|椀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bowl|1=wan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540470}}) are borrowed from mounds, abysses, large rocks, or caves in the shady parts of mountains.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, p.230</ref>
== Examples of the Lending Bowl Legend == {{Quote|In a place called Kanaya, there was a large rock, and when one shouted beside that rock, “Lend me meal trays and bowls for as many people as I need,” the next morning the desired number of trays and bowls were prepared. One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture}} {{ill|Yazu District, Tottori|en|Yazu District, Tottori|WD=Q1043163|lt=Hattō District}}’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}
== Overview == In Japan prior to the early modern period, families did not possess more tableware than was necessary for the household; consequently, during events that gathered large numbers of people, such as weddings, it was not uncommon for meal trays and bowls to be borrowed from elsewhere.
The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okuwa|1=Ōu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446093}} to {{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyushu}}—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region}} and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Kanto|1=Kita Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366730}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Child Deity|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, {{ill|Kappa (folklore)|en|Kappa (folklore)|WD=Q335140|lt=Kappa}}, {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竜|1=dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7559}}, {{ill|Kami|en|Kami|WD=Q524158|lt=Goddess}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ({{ill|Stream pool|en|Stream pool|WD=Q2385513|lt=Abyss}}), waterfalls ({{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洞窟|1=cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q35509}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ({{ill|Hidden village|en|Hidden village|WD=Q45263|lt=Hidden Village}}). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god}}. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}}, {{ill|Inba District|en|Inba District|WD=Q677245|lt=Inba District}}, {{ill|Sakae, Chiba|en|Sakae, Chiba|WD=Q1202754|lt=Sakae Town}} (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in {{ill|Shisui|en|Shisui|WD=Q782157|lt=Shisui Town}}, have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the {{ill|Kijiya|en|Kijiya|WD=Q11518247|lt=Mochishi}}, leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>

In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist {{ill|Torii Ryūzō|en|Torii Ryūzō|WD=Q707532|lt=Ryūzō Torii}} pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}}, it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, {{ill|Shinichiro Kurimoto|en|Shinichiro Kurimoto|WD=Q7497527|lt=Shinichirō Kurimoto}} argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.

The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okuwa|1=Ōu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446093}} to {{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyushu}}—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region}} and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Kanto|1=Kita Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366730}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Child Deity|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, {{ill|Kappa (folklore)|en|Kappa (folklore)|WD=Q335140|lt=Kappa}}, {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竜|1=dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7559}}, {{ill|Kami|en|Kami|WD=Q524158|lt=Goddess}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ({{ill|Stream pool|en|Stream pool|WD=Q2385513|lt=Abyss}}), waterfalls ({{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洞窟|1=cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q35509}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ({{ill|Hidden village|en|Hidden village|WD=Q45263|lt=Hidden Village}}). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god}}. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}}, {{ill|Inba District|en|Inba District|WD=Q677245|lt=Inba District}}, {{ill|Sakae, Chiba|en|Sakae, Chiba|WD=Q1202754|lt=Sakae Town}} (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in {{ill|Shisui|en|Shisui|WD=Q782157|lt=Shisui Town}}, have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Cave Tomb|ja|横穴墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Cave Tomb|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the {{ill|Kijiya|en|Kijiya|WD=Q11518247|lt=Mochishi}}, leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of {{ill|Silent trade|en|Silent trade|WD=Q2986252|lt=Silent Trade}} with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist {{ill|Torii Ryūzō|en|Torii Ryūzō|WD=Q707532|lt=Ryūzō Torii}} pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}}, it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, {{ill|Shinichiro Kurimoto|en|Shinichiro Kurimoto|WD=Q7497527|lt=Shinichirō Kurimoto}} argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year {{ill|Masao Oka|en|Masao Oka|WD=Q2017050|lt=Masao Oka}} argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In {{ill|France|en|France|WD=Q142|lt=France}}, there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a {{ill|Stock pot|en|Stock pot|WD=Q15711817|lt=Pot}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *{{Cite book|和書|last=神野|first=清|chapter=印波国造の奥津城|title=龍女建立-龍角寺古墳群と龍角寺-|publisher={{ill|Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|ja|千葉県立房総のむら|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiba Prefectural Boso no Mura|1=Chiba Prefectural Open-air Museum Boso-no-Mura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406280}}|year=2009|date=2009|ncid=BB00830428|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}}
{{Japan-culture-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椀貸伝説]]
[[File:Wankishi ike cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 22.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtomi Province}} {{ill|Sano District|ja|佐野郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sano District|1=Saya district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11385111}} {{ill|Tomibe Village|ja|富部村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomibe Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s Lending Bowl Legend as depicted in a color painting from the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (from the edition of “Wangan Ike” by {{ill|Fujichōkō|ja|藤長庚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujichōkō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}} in the {{ill|Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja|遠江古蹟圖會|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtomi Ancient Monuments Illustrated Collection|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 兵藤庄右衛門|1=Hyōdō Shōemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110647440}}, 1803; held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library}})]] '''Lending Bowl Legend''' (椀貸伝説, Wankashidensetsu) is a collective term for a type of folk tale and traditional narrative – one of the narrative types of folklore – whose central theme is a story in which meal trays ({{ill|Zen (container)|ja|膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meal Tray|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11612404|lt=Zen}}) or bowls ({{ill|Bowl|ja|椀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bowl|1=wan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540470}}) are borrowed from mounds, abysses, large rocks, or caves in the shady parts of mountains.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, p.230</ref>
== Examples of the Lending Bowl Legend == {{Quote|In a place called Kanaya, there was a large rock, and when one shouted beside that rock, “Lend me meal trays and bowls for as many people as I need,” the next morning the desired number of trays and bowls were prepared. One time, when an ungrateful person returned fewer bowls than he had borrowed, he was never lent any again.|{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture}} {{ill|Yazu District, Tottori|en|Yazu District, Tottori|WD=Q1043163|lt=Hattō District}}’s tradition|『昔話伝説小事典』, みずうみ書房, 1987, p.273}}
== Overview == In Japan prior to the early modern period, families did not possess more tableware than was necessary for the household; consequently, during events that gathered large numbers of people, such as weddings, it was not uncommon for meal trays and bowls to be borrowed from elsewhere.
The Lending Bowl Legend has spread over a wide area of Japan—from {{ill|Okuwa|ja|奥羽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okuwa|1=Ōu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446093}} to {{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyushu}}—but is especially prevalent in the mountainous regions of the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region}} and {{ill|Northern Kanto|ja|北関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Kanto|1=Kita Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366730}}. Although the basic storyline is generally the same as in the example above, there are many minor variations. The lending party may be various beings such as {{ill|Child Deity|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Child Deity|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, {{ill|Kappa (folklore)|en|Kappa (folklore)|WD=Q335140|lt=Kappa}}, {{ill|Dragon|ja|龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竜|1=dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7559}}, {{ill|Kami|en|Kami|WD=Q524158|lt=Goddess}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|お地蔵様|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}, or other mysterious or diverse entities.
The lending locations vary as well; they may be directly visited at abysses ({{ill|Stream pool|en|Stream pool|WD=Q2385513|lt=Abyss}}), waterfalls ({{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}), rocks, or caves ({{ill|Cave|ja|洞穴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洞窟|1=cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q35509}}) in the shady parts of mountains, or even in hidden villages ({{ill|Hidden village|en|Hidden village|WD=Q45263|lt=Hidden Village}}). Comparatively, many of these locations are associated with water, and there are numerous legends that appear to be related to the worship of the {{ill|Water God Shōdōtan|ja|水神少童譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water God Shōdōtan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god}}. In some tales, a relationship is already established through which one may borrow meal trays and bowls, while in others the bowls are seen to flow from upstream in a river, leading to the discovery of an otherworld or hidden village.
In some cases, ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}}, {{ill|Inba District|en|Inba District|WD=Q677245|lt=Inba District}}, {{ill|Sakae, Chiba|en|Sakae, Chiba|WD=Q1202754|lt=Sakae Town}} (see also <ref>{{Sfn|神野|2009|p=16}}</ref>), or the {{ill|Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|ja|カンカンムロ横穴群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|1=Kankanmuro Yokonaketsu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98605801}} in {{ill|Shisui|en|Shisui|WD=Q782157|lt=Shisui Town}}, have become legendary sites for such tales, where horizontal stone chambers ({{ill|Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Subterranean Stone Chamber (Horizontal)|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}) or horizontal cave tombs ({{ill|Horizontal Yokoanabo|ja|横穴墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal Yokoanabo|1=cave tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11543436}}) are featured. In the case of Shisui Town (Kankanmuro), although the initially prayed-to entity for borrowing bowls was {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}, the being actually present within the horizontal cave tomb that lends the trays and bowls remains unidentified.
Some houses or regions retain bowls that were supposedly not returned by the ungrateful, or documents attesting to a default in repayment. Among these items, some hint at a connection with the {{ill|Kijiya|en|Kijiya|WD=Q11518247|lt=Mochishi}}, leading to the interpretation that the legends originated from recitations ({{ill|Verbal Formula|ja|口上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Verbal Formula|qq=}}) heard during trade with these craftsmen.<ref>『神話伝説辞典』, 東京堂出版, 1968, 第6刷, pp.471–472</ref> In regions where meal trays and bowls are considered communal property of the village, the tales are also viewed as cautionary narratives that admonish against stealing or breaking borrowed items.<ref>柳田國男『定本 柳田國男集 第五巻』, 筑摩書房, 1977, 第14刷, pp.238–239</ref>
== On the “Silent Trade” Theory == There is controversy among folklorists regarding the theory that the Lending Bowl Legend represents a form of {{ill|Silent trade|en|Silent trade|WD=Q2986252|lt=Silent Trade}} with foreigners. In 1917 (the 6th year of the Taishō period), the anthropologist {{ill|Torii Ryūzō|en|Torii Ryūzō|WD=Q707532|lt=Ryūzō Torii}} pointed out in the ''Anthropological Journal'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is a “silent trade” – that is, a trade conducted without the parties coming into contact or exchanging words (i.e., a translated term for “Silent trade”). In response, in 1918 (Taishō 7), {{ill|Kunio Yanagida|ja|柳田国男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunio Yanagida|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳田國男|1=Kunio Yanagita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971687}} published an article titled “Hidden Village” in the ''Tokyo Nichinichi Shimbun'', arguing that the Lending Bowl Legend is merely a lending-borrowing relationship and, furthermore, that since the counterpart is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}}, it is a phenomenon of faith—a form of expression of the “otherworldly concept” similar to the Dragon Palace Legend and hidden village legends.
After the war, in 1954 (Showa 29), {{ill|Toshio Kitami|ja|北見俊夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshio Kitami|1=Kitami Toshio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15135854}} compiled cases of the Lending Bowl Legend from across Japan and denied that it constituted a “silent trade”. On the other hand, in 1979, {{ill|Shinichiro Kurimoto|en|Shinichiro Kurimoto|WD=Q7497527|lt=Shinichirō Kurimoto}} argued in ''Economic Anthropology'' that the Lending Bowl Legend is indeed a “silent trade”, representing a primitive form of commerce.
Regarding the view that “silent trade” is the “primordial form of trade”, in the same year {{ill|Masao Oka|en|Masao Oka|WD=Q2017050|lt=Masao Oka}} argued in ''Ijin Sonota'' that “silent trade” is not a primitive form of trade but merely a special type of exchange—a custom of friendly gift-giving predicated on the hospitality extended to guests.
== Worldwide Lending Bowl Legends == In {{ill|France|en|France|WD=Q142|lt=France}}, there is a story in which one beseeches a mound to lend a {{ill|Stock pot|en|Stock pot|WD=Q15711817|lt=Pot}}.
